scenes.title: The Follow-Up Visit
scenes.content:
A 9-year-old boy with moderate persistent asthma returns for follow-up. Despite being prescribed fluticasone MDI 110 mcg twice daily, his asthma remains poorly controlled with nighttime awakenings 3×/week.

HR88
RR18
SpO297%
scenes.hint_beginner: What are common reasons for poor asthma control despite appropriate prescriptions?
scenes.hint_advanced: Consider the difference between medication adherence and medication delivery efficiency.

question.prompt: What is the MOST likely reason for this patient's poor control?

options.text: The fluticasone dose is too low
options.explanation: Dose is appropriate for moderate persistent asthma.

options.text: Poor inhaler technique leading to inadequate drug delivery
He needs a long-acting beta-agonist added
options.explanation: Before stepping up, always verify technique and adherence.

options.text: Fluticasone is the wrong ICS for children
options.explanation: Fluticasone is appropriate for pediatric use.

explanation.correct: Correct! Studies show up to 70-90% of patients use MDIs incorrectly. Before stepping up therapy, always assess and correct inhaler technique — it's the most common cause of apparent treatment failure.
explanation.incorrect: Poor inhaler technique is the most common and correctable cause of uncontrolled asthma. Always verify technique before escalating therapy.

question.teaching:
📋 Inhaler Technique Errors
Most common MDI errors:
1. Failure to coordinate actuation with inhalation
2. Inadequate breath-hold (<5 seconds)
3. Breathing too fast (should be slow, steady)
4. Not shaking MDI before use
5. Not using a spacer (especially in children)

Impact: Up to 90% of patients demonstrate ≥1 critical error → drug deposits in oropharynx instead of lungs.

why_chain.q: Why does poor technique matter so much?
why_chain.a: Without proper coordination, only 10-20% of the dose reaches the lungs. The rest deposits in the mouth/throat, causing local side effects (thrush) without therapeutic benefit.

why_chain.q: At what age can children use MDIs without spacers?
why_chain.a: Most guidelines recommend spacers for all ages with MDIs. By age 5-6, children can begin coordinating, but spacers improve delivery at any age.

scenes.title: The Technique Assessment
scenes.content: You ask the patient to demonstrate his inhaler technique. He shakes the MDI, brings it to his lips, presses the canister, and takes a quick breath in. He does not use a spacer and does not hold his breath afterward.
scenes.hint_beginner: How many errors can you identify?
scenes.hint_advanced: Think about the physics of aerosol deposition — particle size, velocity, and airway anatomy.

question.prompt: Which error has the GREATEST impact on drug delivery to the lower airways?

options.explanation: He did shake it — this is important but not the biggest error here.
Fast inhalation is a significant error but not the most impactful single change.

options.text: Not using a valved holding chamber (spacer)

options.explanation: Important but secondary to getting the drug into the lungs in the first place.

explanation.correct: Correct! A spacer eliminates the need for coordination between actuation and inhalation, reduces oropharyngeal deposition by 80%, and increases lung deposition from ~10% to ~20%. For children, it is the single most important intervention.
explanation.incorrect: A valved holding chamber (spacer) provides the biggest improvement in drug delivery for MDI users, especially children.

question.teaching:
📋 Spacer Benefits
Spacer/VHC advantages:
• Eliminates need for hand-breath coordination
• Reduces oropharyngeal deposition by ~80%
• Increases lung deposition from ~10% to ~20%
• Reduces risk of oral candidiasis with ICS
• Reduces systemic absorption of ICS

Types: Valved holding chambers (preferred), simple tube spacers. Anti-static spacers improve delivery further.

why_chain.q: Why does fast inhalation reduce delivery?
why_chain.a: Fast airflow increases inertial impaction in the oropharynx. Slow, steady inhalation (about 3-5 seconds) allows smaller particles to travel deeper into the bronchial tree.

scenes.title: Choosing the Right Device
scenes.content: The mother asks about switching to a dry powder inhaler (DPI) since her son finds the MDI frustrating. She has also heard about nebulizers.
scenes.hint_beginner: What are the key differences between MDI, DPI, and nebulizer delivery?
scenes.hint_advanced: Consider the inspiratory flow requirements for each device and how that relates to a 9-year-old's respiratory mechanics.

question.prompt: What is the minimum inspiratory flow rate typically required for effective DPI use?

options.explanation: Too low — this is adequate for MDI+spacer but not most DPIs.
Some newer DPIs work at this flow, but most require higher.
Only needed for high-resistance DPIs; standard threshold is lower.

explanation.correct: Correct! Most DPIs require an inspiratory flow of ≥60 L/min to adequately deaggregate the powder. Children under 6 often cannot generate sufficient flow. This 9-year-old likely can, but technique should be verified with an In-Check device.
explanation.incorrect: DPIs require adequate inspiratory flow (typically ≥60 L/min). Always verify the patient can generate sufficient flow before switching.

question.teaching:
📋 Device Comparison
MDI + Spacer: Best for young children, requires no inspiratory effort, most studied
DPI: Breath-actuated (no coordination needed), requires ≥60 L/min flow, humidity-sensitive
SMI (Respimat): Slow mist, good deposition, no propellant
Nebulizer: No technique needed, time-consuming (10-15 min), useful in acute settings or very young children

why_chain.q: Can DPIs be used during acute exacerbations?
why_chain.a: Generally no — during exacerbations, patients may not generate adequate inspiratory flow. MDI+spacer or nebulizer preferred in acute settings.

scenes.title: Correcting the Technique
scenes.content: You decide to keep the MDI but add a spacer and reteach technique. You demonstrate the correct steps and ask the patient to practice.
scenes.hint_beginner: What are the correct steps for MDI + spacer use?
scenes.hint_advanced: Consider what happens if the patient actuates more than one puff into the spacer at a time.

question.prompt: What is the correct number of puffs to actuate into the spacer at one time?

options.text: One puff at a time, with full inhalation between each
Two puffs simultaneously to save time
options.explanation: Multiple actuations into a spacer cause drug to clump and reduces delivery.

options.text: All prescribed puffs at once, then breathe normally
options.explanation: This defeats the purpose of the spacer.

options.text: It does not matter — the spacer holds all the medication
options.explanation: Spacer volume is limited; multiple puffs cause particle aggregation.

explanation.correct: Correct! Always one puff at a time into the spacer, followed by slow deep inhalation and 10-second breath hold (or tidal breathing ×4-6 breaths for young children).
explanation.incorrect: Only actuate one puff at a time into the spacer. Multiple simultaneous puffs reduce delivery through particle aggregation.

question.teaching:
📋 Correct MDI + Spacer Steps
1. Remove cap, shake MDI well (5 seconds)
2. Insert MDI into spacer
3. Exhale fully (away from spacer)
4. Place mouthpiece in mouth, seal lips
5. Actuate ONE puff
6. Inhale slowly and deeply (3-5 seconds)
7. Hold breath 10 seconds (or tidal breathe ×4-6)
8. Wait 30-60 seconds before next puff
9. Rinse mouth after ICS use

why_chain.a: Inhaled corticosteroids deposited in the oropharynx cause local immunosuppression → oral candidiasis (thrush) and dysphonia. Rinsing removes deposited drug.

scenes.title: The Spacer Problem
scenes.content: At the 4-week follow-up, the mother reports the spacer makes a whistling sound when her son inhales. His nighttime symptoms have improved but not resolved.
scenes.hint_beginner: What does the whistling sound from the spacer indicate?
scenes.hint_advanced: Consider what happens to drug delivery when the flow indicator whistle activates.

question.prompt: What does the whistle sound in a valved holding chamber indicate?

options.text: The spacer is broken and needs replacement
options.explanation: The whistle is a built-in feature, not a defect.

options.text: The patient is inhaling too fast — the whistle is a flow indicator
The valve is stuck and the medication cannot exit
options.explanation: A stuck valve would prevent any inhalation, not produce a whistle.
Medication status has no effect on spacer sounds.

explanation.correct: Correct! Many VHCs have built-in flow indicators that whistle when inspiratory flow exceeds the optimal range. The whistle means the patient is breathing too fast, increasing oropharyngeal impaction and reducing lung deposition.
explanation.incorrect: The spacer whistle is a flow feedback tool — it means the patient is inhaling too fast and should slow down to optimize drug delivery.

question.teaching:
📋 Spacer Troubleshooting
Whistle = too fast: Coach slower inhalation until no whistle
Valve stuck: Wash with warm soapy water, air dry (do NOT towel dry — static charge attracts drug particles)
Static charge: New plastic spacers accumulate static → prime by washing with dilute detergent, air dry before first use
Replace: Every 6-12 months or if cracked

why_chain.a: Electrostatic charge on plastic spacer walls attracts aerosolized drug particles, reducing the dose available for inhalation by up to 50%. Washing with detergent dissipates static.

scenes.title: Reassessment and Step Therapy
scenes.content: After correcting technique (slow inhalation, no whistle), you reassess in 4 more weeks. Nighttime symptoms now only 1×/month. Daytime symptoms well controlled. FEV1 improved from 78% to 92% predicted.
scenes.hint_beginner: When is it appropriate to consider stepping down therapy?
scenes.hint_advanced: What is the minimum duration of well-controlled asthma before step-down is recommended?

question.prompt: When should you consider stepping down this patient's therapy?

options.explanation: Need at least 3 months of sustained control before step-down.

options.text: After 3 months of well-controlled asthma, attempt step-down
Never step down once control is achieved
options.explanation: Overtreatment carries risks; step-down should always be attempted.

options.text: Switch directly to PRN albuterol only
options.explanation: Skipping steps risks loss of control; step down gradually.

explanation.correct: Correct! NAEPP/GINA guidelines recommend maintaining well-controlled asthma for at least 3 months before attempting step-down. Step down gradually (e.g., reduce ICS dose by 25-50%), monitor for 4-6 weeks at each step.
explanation.incorrect: Step-down should be attempted after ≥3 months of well-controlled asthma. Step down gradually and monitor at each level.

question.teaching:
📋 Step-Down Principles
When: ≥3 months well-controlled asthma
How: Reduce ICS dose by 25-50% OR remove add-on therapy
Monitor: 4-6 weeks at each step-down level
Step back up: If control worsens, return to previous step
Goal: Find minimum effective therapy that maintains control

Key principle: Technique correction alone may provide the equivalent of stepping up therapy — always check before adding medications.

why_chain.q: What's the risk of never stepping down?
why_chain.a: Chronic unnecessary ICS exposure in children risks growth suppression (average 0.5-1 cm reduction in final adult height with medium-dose ICS), adrenal suppression, and decreased bone density.

scenes.title: The Flushing Patient
scenes.content:
A 38-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of flushing, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, and near-syncope for 6 months. Episodes are unpredictable but sometimes triggered by hot showers, alcohol, or NSAIDs. She was recently stung by a wasp and had a severe anaphylactic reaction.

BP102/64
HR98
RR18
SpO298%
Temp37.0°C
scenes.hint_beginner: What conditions can cause episodic flushing with systemic symptoms?
scenes.hint_advanced: Compare the mediator profiles: histamine (mast cells) vs serotonin (carcinoid) vs catecholamines (pheochromocytoma).

question.prompt: Which feature most strongly suggests a mast cell disorder over carcinoid syndrome?

options.explanation: Both cause flushing.
Both can cause GI symptoms.

options.text: Severe anaphylaxis to Hymenoptera sting in an adult

options.explanation: Both can cause hemodynamic instability.

explanation.correct: Correct! Severe anaphylaxis to Hymenoptera venom in adults should always raise suspicion for underlying mastocytosis/MCAS. Up to 7% of adults with severe venom anaphylaxis have underlying clonal mast cell disease.
explanation.incorrect: Severe Hymenoptera anaphylaxis in adults is a red flag for underlying mast cell disease — always test tryptase.

question.teaching:
📋 Mast Cell Disorder Clues
Red flags for mastocytosis/MCAS:
• Recurrent anaphylaxis (especially to venom)
• Flushing + hypotension (not just flushing)
• GI symptoms (cramping, diarrhea)
• Urticaria pigmentosa (brown macules)
• Osteoporosis in young patients
• Triggers: heat, alcohol, NSAIDs, opioids, exercise

Key distinction from carcinoid: Carcinoid flushing is typically DRY; mast cell flushing is often WET (with urticaria).

why_chain.a: NSAIDs inhibit COX-1, shunting arachidonic acid toward the lipoxygenase pathway → increased leukotriene production → mast cell activation. Some patients tolerate COX-2 selective agents.

scenes.title: The Diagnostic Workup
scenes.content: Physical exam reveals several tan-brown macules on the trunk that urticate when stroked (positive Darier sign). You suspect a mast cell disorder.
scenes.hint_beginner: What is the Darier sign and what does it indicate?
scenes.hint_advanced: Consider which serum and urine biomarkers reflect mast cell burden vs activation.

question.prompt: What is the MOST important initial lab test when evaluating for systemic mastocytosis?

options.explanation: Useful but not first-line; less specific than tryptase.
Important for staging but not diagnostic.
Not specific for mast cell disease.

explanation.correct: Correct! Baseline serum tryptase is the most important initial test. Persistently elevated tryptase (>20 ng/mL) suggests increased mast cell burden and is a minor WHO criterion for systemic mastocytosis.
explanation.incorrect: Serum tryptase is the cornerstone initial test — a persistently elevated baseline (>20 ng/mL) strongly suggests systemic mast cell disease.

question.teaching:
📋 Diagnostic Algorithm
Step 1: Baseline serum tryptase (>20 ng/mL = minor criterion)
Step 2: 24-hour urine: N-methylhistamine, 11β-prostaglandin F2α
Step 3: Bone marrow biopsy (if tryptase elevated) — look for mast cell aggregates, spindle-shaped morphology, CD25+/CD2+ aberrant markers
Step 4: KIT D816V mutation testing

WHO criteria for SM: 1 major + 1 minor, OR 3 minor criteria. Major = multifocal dense BM mast cell aggregates (≥15 cells).

why_chain.a: Mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS) can occur with normal baseline tryptase. Document a rise of ≥20% + 2 ng/mL during an episode. Consider urine metabolites for diagnosis.

scenes.title: Bone Marrow Results
scenes.content: Serum tryptase returns at 42 ng/mL. Bone marrow biopsy shows multifocal aggregates of spindle-shaped mast cells (>15 per cluster), CD25+, and KIT D816V mutation is detected.
scenes.hint_beginner: Does this meet criteria for systemic mastocytosis?
scenes.hint_advanced: Review the WHO major and minor criteria and how many are needed for diagnosis.

question.prompt: How many WHO diagnostic criteria does this patient meet?

options.explanation: Count again — she meets more.
Check all five criteria against her findings.
Serum tryptase must be >20 to count — it is (42 ng/mL), but re-examine if mast cell morphology >25% spindle-shaped is confirmed as separate.

explanation.correct: Correct! Major: multifocal dense aggregates (≥15 mast cells). Minor: spindle-shaped morphology, CD25+ expression, KIT D816V mutation, tryptase >20 ng/mL. She meets 1 major + 3 minor — well exceeding the diagnostic threshold.
explanation.incorrect: This patient clearly meets WHO criteria: 1 major + 3 minor criteria. Only 1 major + 1 minor (or 3 minor alone) is needed.

question.teaching:
📋 WHO Criteria for Systemic Mastocytosis
MAJOR: Multifocal dense mast cell aggregates (≥15 cells) in BM or extracutaneous tissue

MINOR (need 1 if major present, or 3 alone):
1. >25% spindle-shaped mast cells in BM
2. KIT D816V mutation
3. CD25+ and/or CD2+ expression on mast cells
4. Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL

Diagnosis: 1 major + 1 minor, OR 3 minor criteria

why_chain.q: What does KIT D816V mutation mean therapeutically?
why_chain.a: D816V confers resistance to imatinib (which works on wild-type KIT). Midostaurin and avapritinib are effective against D816V-mutated mast cells.

scenes.title: Classification and Prognosis
scenes.content: With the diagnosis confirmed, you need to determine the subtype. CBC shows normal counts. No hepatosplenomegaly. No evidence of organ dysfunction (B-findings or C-findings). Skin lesions present.
scenes.hint_beginner: What subtype categories exist for systemic mastocytosis?
scenes.hint_advanced: B-findings indicate high mast cell burden; C-findings indicate organ damage. How does their absence guide classification?

question.prompt: What subtype of systemic mastocytosis does this patient have?

options.explanation: ASM requires C-findings (organ damage).
Requires >20% mast cells in peripheral blood or >30% in BM aspirate.
SSM requires 2+ B-findings without C-findings.

explanation.correct: Correct! Indolent SM (ISM): meets SM criteria, no B-findings or C-findings, no associated hematologic neoplasm. This is the most common subtype (~80%) and has an excellent prognosis (normal life expectancy).
explanation.incorrect: No organ damage (C-findings) and no high-burden markers (B-findings) = indolent SM, the most common and most favorable subtype.

question.teaching:
📋 SM Classification
Indolent SM (ISM): No B/C-findings. Best prognosis.
Smoldering SM (SSM): ≥2 B-findings, no C-findings
Aggressive SM (ASM): ≥1 C-finding (cytopenias, hepatomegaly with dysfunction, skeletal lesions, malabsorption)
SM-AHN: SM + associated hematologic neoplasm
Mast Cell Leukemia: ≥20% mast cells in blood

B-findings: BM infiltration >30%, tryptase >200, hepatosplenomegaly, dysplasia
C-findings: Organ damage from mast cell infiltration

why_chain.q: What's the life expectancy for ISM?
why_chain.a: Near-normal life expectancy. The main morbidity is from mediator-release symptoms (anaphylaxis risk) rather than organ infiltration.

scenes.title: Mediator Management
scenes.content: You need to initiate therapy to control her mediator-release symptoms (flushing, GI symptoms, anaphylaxis risk).
scenes.hint_beginner: What are the pillars of pharmacologic management for ISM?
scenes.hint_advanced: Think about blocking histamine at both H1 and H2 receptors, plus mast cell stabilization.

question.prompt: What is the foundation of pharmacologic therapy for indolent SM symptoms?

options.explanation: Cytoreductive therapy is reserved for advanced SM, not ISM symptom management.
Not first-line for chronic management; used for acute flares.
May help IgE-mediated symptoms but is not first-line for ISM.

explanation.correct: Correct! H1 blocker (cetirizine, fexofenadine) + H2 blocker (famotidine) form the backbone of ISM management. Add cromolyn sodium for GI symptoms. Leukotriene inhibitor (montelukast) for flushing. Epinephrine autoinjector is mandatory.
explanation.incorrect: Dual antihistamine therapy (H1 + H2) is the foundation. Every patient with SM must also carry epinephrine autoinjectors.

question.teaching:
📋 ISM Management Ladder
Tier 1 (All patients):
• H1 antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg BID or fexofenadine)
• H2 antihistamine (famotidine 20 mg BID)
• Epinephrine autoinjector (2 doses at all times)

Tier 2 (Add-on):
• Cromolyn sodium (GI symptoms)
• Montelukast (flushing)
• Aspirin (if tolerated — for flushing/vascular symptoms)

Tier 3: Omalizumab for refractory anaphylaxis
Tier 4: Cytoreductive therapy (only for advanced SM)

why_chain.a: Mast cells release histamine that acts on both H1 (skin, airways) and H2 (gastric acid, cardiac) receptors. Blocking both provides broader symptom control. H2 blockers also reduce GI symptoms and acid hypersecretion.

scenes.title: Anaphylaxis Prevention
scenes.content: The patient asks about venom immunotherapy since her anaphylaxis was triggered by a wasp sting. She is anxious about outdoor activities.
scenes.hint_beginner: Is venom immunotherapy (VIT) recommended in mastocytosis?
scenes.hint_advanced: Consider the evidence for VIT in clonal mast cell disease — does it work the same as in patients without mastocytosis?

question.prompt: What is the recommendation for venom immunotherapy in this patient?

options.text: VIT is contraindicated in mastocytosis due to high reaction risk
options.explanation: VIT is actually strongly recommended despite the risk.

options.text: Lifelong VIT is recommended — do not discontinue after the standard 5 years
VIT for the standard 5 years, then reassess
options.explanation: In mastocytosis, stopping VIT carries significant relapse risk.

options.text: VIT is unnecessary if she carries epinephrine
options.explanation: Epinephrine treats reactions but VIT prevents them.

explanation.correct: Correct! VIT is strongly recommended AND should be continued indefinitely in patients with mastocytosis. Unlike the general population where 5 years is standard, mastocytosis patients have a high relapse rate (up to 30-40%) after VIT discontinuation.
explanation.incorrect: Lifelong VIT is recommended for mastocytosis patients with Hymenoptera anaphylaxis — do NOT stop after the standard 5-year course.

question.teaching:
📋 VIT in Mastocytosis
Indication: All mastocytosis patients with Hymenoptera anaphylaxis
Duration: LIFELONG (vs 5 years in general population)
Efficacy: Reduces reaction risk from ~60% to ~10% per sting
Precautions: Premedicate with antihistamines, have epinephrine ready, consider extended observation

Additional prevention:
• Medical alert bracelet
• Avoid triggers (alcohol, NSAIDs, extreme heat)
• Pre-procedure protocols (surgery, contrast)

why_chain.a: The underlying clonal mast cell population persists indefinitely. Without ongoing VIT, the risk of severe anaphylaxis to future stings returns. Stopping VIT is associated with 30-40% relapse rate in mastocytosis vs <5% in general population.
